title: Case Conversion
html:
Case Conversion

Case Conversion

You can use ProcScript statements and functions to convert string from uppercase to lowercase or from lowercase to uppercase. The default conversion behavior is determined by the values of NLS assignment settings and ProcScript functions, which control locale-based processing.

The following ProcScript instructions are available for converting between uppercase and lowercase:

  • lowercase (deprecated)
  • uppercase (deprecated)
  • $lowercase
  • $uppercase

When using syntax strings to search for a pattern of characters, you can specify whether the search should be case-sensitive or case-insensitive. For more information, see $syntax and Syntax Strings for Pattern Matching.

Default Behavior and Overrides

Note: The way in which $uppercase and $lowercase convert case depends on the current NLS settings, whereas lowercase and uppercase are not influenced by NLS settings.

The behavior for $uppercase and $lowercase is determined by:

  1. The value of the second argument, if specified. If it is classic, locale-based conversion rules are not applied. If it is nlslocale, the current value of $nlslocale is used.
  2. If the second argument is not specified, the value of $nlscase is used, as set by the $NLS_CASE assignment setting or $nlscase ProcScript function.
  3. If $nlscase is not set, the value of $nlslocale is used, as set by $NLS_LOCALE or $nlslocale.
  4. If $nlslocale is not set, or is set to classic, the conversion is done based on the Unicode upper- and lowercase equivalents.
  5. If $nlslocale is set to a specific locale (language and country), locale-based rules are applied during case conversion.

For example, if $NLS_LOCALE is set to system, you can set $NLS_CASE to classic to prevent locale-based rules from being applied when converting case.

It is also possible to turn locale-based conversion on or off by setting a qualifier in $lowercase or $uppercase. For example:

$nlslocale = "tr_TR" ; set locale to Turkish(Turkey)
FIELD2 = $lowercase ("KIPIRTI")
Result: FIELD2 = kıpırtı ; default is to use locale, when it is set
FIELD1 = $lowercase ("KIPIRTI", "classic")
;Result: FIELD1 = kipirti ; locale is overridden

Locale-Based Case Conversion

Locale-based rules allow case folding, meaning that a single character may be converted to multiple characters. For example, the German lowercase character ß would be converted to uppercase SS. Special characters in languages such as Turkish, Azerbaijani, and Lithuanian are correctly converted. For example, Turkish uses both a dotted I and an undotted ı, which have dotted and undotted uppercase equivalents (İ and I).

If the NLS locale is set to classic, no locale-based conversion rules are applied and character conversion is character-by-character according to Unicode definitions. This is equivalent to the behavior prior to Uniface 9.4.01.

Related Links
  • Data Handling in ProcScript

    The ProcScript language enables you to convert data from one type to another, perform arithmetic operations, extract data values, and perform string substitution. Uniface carries out implicit type conversion whenever necessary, for example, when assignments are made between non-matching data types or when comparing non-matching data types. The ProcScript language also provides a number of functions that allow you to convert from one data type to another.

  • Language and Locale

    The language of an application is associated not only with a character set, but with regional conventions for sorting, for expressing numbers, currency, date and time, and so on. The combination of language and region is known as locale.
